perturbation.original_text: 1.3 "Hosting Servers" shall mean those servers (including both the search engine cluster and the crawling cluster) and other hardware and third party software identified in the Deployment, Hosting and Maintenance Specifications that shall be used to host or service the Microsoft Search Engine and Usage Data.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.3 "Hosting Servers" shall mean those servers (excluding both the search engine cluster and the crawling cluster) and other hardware and third party software identified in the Deployment, Hosting and Maintenance Specifications that shall be used to host or service the Microsoft Search Engine and Usage Data.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "Hosting Servers" is changed to exclude the search engine and crawling clusters. This creates an in-text contradiction because later sections (e.g., Section 2.6) refer to the 'Hosting Servers' being used to service the Microsoft Search Engine, which inherently depends on the search engine cluster. This exclusion makes it ambiguous whether the contract intends for the search engine and crawling clusters to be considered part of the infrastructure Inktomi is responsible for maintaining.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.6 Microsoft will have the right, in its sole and absolute discretion, to require Inktomi to devote a separate cluster of Hosting Servers to servicing Microsoft hereunder, which Servers would not be used to service any needs of Inktomi and/or any third parties. The Hosting Servers purchased by Inktomi at Microsoft's request hereunder would be re-deployed to such separate cluster.
perturbation.justification: The modified text redefines "Hosting Servers" to explicitly exclude "the search engine cluster and the crawling cluster." However, section 2.6 states that Microsoft can require Inktomi to devote a cluster of Hosting Servers to servicing Microsoft, implying that 'Hosting Servers' *do* include the infrastructure necessary to service Microsoft, which includes the search engine cluster. This creates a direct contradiction because the definition now excludes components that are later assumed to be included, making the contract internally inconsistent.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 Inktomi shall strictly adhere to all Security Measures in performing the Services, including without limitation securing the Usage Data, which it may possess or have under its control from time to time, from unauthorized access and modification.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 Inktomi shall strictly adhere to all Security Protocols in performing the Services, including without limitation securing the Usage Data, which it may possess or have under its control from time to time, from unauthorized access and modification.
perturbation.explanation: The term "Security Measures" is replaced with "Security Protocols". "Security Measures" is defined in Section 1.9 as 'those procedures and precautions described in Exhibit A'. By changing the terminology without explicitly stating that 'Security Protocols' is interchangeable with 'Security Measures', it introduces ambiguity. If the contract only defines 'Security Measures' and not 'Security Protocols', it is unclear whether Inktomi is now bound by a different, undefined standard of security.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3.2 Microsoft acknowledges that, pursuant to Inktomi's contractual arrangement with its subcontractor, Exodus Communications, Inc. ("Exodus"), Inktomi will locate the Hosting Servers at the facilities of Exodus, and Exodus will provide power and Internet telecommunications services to the Hosting Servers. However, Microsoft will have no obligations or liabilities to Exodus, Inktomi will remain liable for providing all 6 Services to Microsoft notwithstanding its arrangement with Exodus, and Inktomi will [*] and [*] against from any and all [*] to [*] (in accordance with the procedures specified in Section [*] below). A copy of the contract(s) between Inktomi and Exodus is/are attached hereto as Exhibit [*], and Inktomi shall not modify said contract(s) or replace Exodus as its subcontractor for the applicable services (including without limitation by having Inktomi perform the Services directly) without Microsoft's prior written approval (which approval Microsoft agrees to not unreasonably withhold). Inktomi shall provide Exodus with a copy of the Security Measures applicable under this Agreement and will use commercially reasonable efforts to ensure that Exodus strictly adheres to all such Security Measures.
perturbation.justification: The change from "Security Measures" to "Security Protocols" creates a contradiction because Section 3.2 explicitly states that Inktomi shall provide Exodus with a copy of the "Security Measures" applicable under the Agreement and ensure Exodus adheres to them. Since "Security Measures" is defined in Section 1.9 and the modified text replaces it with the undefined "Security Protocols", it is unclear whether Exodus is now bound by the same standard. This introduces ambiguity and potential unenforceability as it is not clear whether Exodus is bound to 'Security Measures' as defined, or some other unknown standard.

perturbation.original_text: 10.2 Microsoft may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon [*] ([*]) days prior written notice. Upon receipt of such notice, Inktomi will discontinue all work hereunder. If Microsoft terminates this Agreement without cause pursuant to this Section 10.2, then Microsoft will pay for all services provided by Inktomi up until the date of termination under this Section 10.2.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.2 Microsoft may terminate this Agreement at any time without cause upon [*] ([*]) days prior written notification. Upon receipt of such notification, Inktomi will discontinue all work hereunder. If Microsoft terminates this Agreement without cause pursuant to this Section 10.2, then Microsoft will pay for all services provided by Inktomi up until the date of termination under this Section 10.2.
perturbation.explanation: The term 'written notice' is replaced by 'written notification'. Section 12.2 details the specific requirements for giving 'notice' under the agreement, including methods of delivery and addresses. By using 'notification' instead of 'notice' in the termination clause, it creates ambiguity as to whether Microsoft must adhere to the procedures outlined in Section 12.2 for termination to be effective. This can lead to disputes over whether proper notification was given, potentially invalidating the termination.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12.2 Any notice required or permitted to be given under this Agreement shall be made in writing and shall be deemed to have been given or made if it is in writing and is: (i) delivered in person, (ii) sent by same day or overnight courier, (iii) mailed by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, postage prepaid, addressed to the party at its address set forth below or at such other address as such party may subsequently furnish to the other party by notice hereunder, or (iv) delivered by facsimile, the transmittal of which shall be confirmed by a telephone call to the other party and by dispatch of a confirming copy of the transmittal by registered or certified mail, postage prepaid. Notices will be deemed effective on the date of delivery in the case of personal delivery, or three (3) business days after mailing, or on the date of dispatch in the case of notification by facsimile (assuming confirmation of transmission). The parties' addresses for purposes of notice shall be as set forth above, provided that all notices to Inktomi shall be sent to the 16 [*]=CERTAIN INFORMATION IN THIS EXHIBIT HAS BEEN OMITTED AND FILED SEPARATELY WITH THE COMMISSION. CONFIDENTIAL TREATMENT HAS BEEN REQUESTED WITH RESPECT TO THE OMITTED PORTIONS. attention of General Counsel; and all notices to Microsoft shall be sent to the attention of Shirish Nadkarni, with a copy to: Law & Corporate Affairs, U.S. Legal.
perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'written notice,' which is specifically defined in Section 12.2 regarding delivery methods and addresses. The changed text replaces 'notice' with 'notification'. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because 'notification' is not a defined term, thus rendering section 12.2 potentially inapplicable and creating ambiguity regarding how Microsoft can properly terminate the agreement. This undermines the explicit procedures outlined in Section 12.2, thus creating a conflict and uncertainty.
